definition: Inflation passthrough from shocks to CPI is regime-dependent and non-linear with a 2-3x multiplier between anchored conditions (0.1-0.5pp) and de-anchored conditions (0.5-1.5pp), making linear models structurally wrong and small shocks capable of outsized effects near the regime boundary.
evidence: 30% agricultural shock produces 0.3-0.5pp in DM under anchored vs. 0.8-1.5pp under unanchored conditions
Breakeven passthrough is 0.1-0.3pp when anchored vs. 0.5-0.8pp when de-anchored
Moderate inflation (2-4%) is credit-neutral-to-positive; above 4% becomes credit-destructive
Builds on highest-confidence prior knowledge base finding at confidence 9
Cross-country panel data across 40+ economies (Carrière-Swallow et al. 2016, Forbes 2019)
Replicated across sub-sample analysis (pre/post-GFC), alternative inflation measures, and different shock identification strategies
Both quant agents independently converge at confidence 9 — the only claim where this occurs
No agent challenged it; no debate weakened it
open_questions: Where exactly is the regime boundary between anchored and de-anchored passthrough?
Is the current 'anchored but strained' regime closer to the boundary than markets price?
Where is the regime boundary located in real time?
Can the boundary be identified with sufficient lead time to be actionable for portfolio positioning?
What observable indicators best predict proximity to the boundary?
